risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Consented individuals were asked to complete baseline assessment visit (carbon monoxide (CO) breath testing and self-report measures) and then undergo randomization via random draws to either the Control Group (Quit Line + 4 weeks Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT)) or MTS (MTS + 4 weeks NRT)." Comment: The study explicitly describes allocation by "random draws," which indicates a random method akin to drawing lots rather than a predictable or quasi-random rule.

Quote: "Consented individuals were asked to complete baseline assessment visit ... and then undergo randomization via random draws to either the Control Group ... or MTS ..." Comment: The report describes how participants were randomized only as "random draws" and does not explicitly state any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g. central randomization, pharmacy control, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes). Because the paper does not explain whether the person enrolling participants could foresee the upcoming assignment before enrollment, allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All potential participants attended a study orientation meeting where both arms of the study were described. Blinding was not used because many participants worked odd hours, had transportation issues or temporary housing, and it was necessary to allow them to decline participation prior to randomization if they could not make the MTS schedule." Comment: This is explicit positive evidence that participants and study personnel were not blinded. Knowledge of assignment could plausibly affect important outcomes in this study, especially self-reported outcomes (e.g. DERS, ACS, FFMQ, course evaluations) and behaviorally influenced smoking cessation outcomes in a behavioral intervention trial. Therefore performance bias is at high risk overall.

Quote: "Blinding was not used because many participants worked odd hours, had transportation issues or temporary housing..." and "Smoking abstinence and questionnaire data were collected at three assessment visits... The primary outcome in this study was 7-day point-prevalence abstinence... confirmed by a Carbon Monoxide (CO) breath test and TLFB." Also, "The following self-report measures were obtained at various time points... Difficulty in Emotion Regulation Scale (DERS)... Attentional Control Scale (ACS)... Five-Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire (FFMQ)..." Comment: Outcome assessment was performed by a mix of patient self-report (the patient is the assessor for TLFB and multiple questionnaire outcomes) and objective testing (CO breath test). Because participants were explicitly not blinded, and the study included subjective self-reported outcomes that could be influenced by knowledge of allocation, the worst-outcome rule gives an overall judgement of High risk, even though CO confirmation is objective.

Quote: "Data analyzed on repeated self-report measures for time-by-group interactions was analyzed for report completers only. This analysis excluded participants who missed 4- or 24-week assessment visits respectively." and Table 4: "MTS Group (n = 38) ... Control Group (n = 43)"; Table 3 footnote: "Intent-to-Treat: (MTS = 105, Control = 91)." Comment: For the repeated self-report outcomes, only completers were analyzed: 38/105 in MTS and 43/91 in control contributed to the Table 4 analyses, implying substantial post-randomization loss on the analyzed outcome (~64% vs ~53%; differential ~11 percentage points). This crosses high-risk thresholds, and the analysis departs from randomization by using completers only. By contrast, smoking abstinence was handled by ITT ("failure to attend the 4- or 24-week assessment visits resulted in coding the participant as relapsed"), but for the self-report outcomes of interest there is positive evidence of high attrition bias.
